positional_bias.notes: Derived positions from quiz.gift match sidecar correct_position_distribution exactly. max_share=0.40 is not strictly greater than 0.40; pass boundary is non-strict per skill spec.

length_bias.notes: All five correct answers fall below 120% of their respective mean distractor lengths. Prior length-bias fixes (q1: 190→152, q3: 180→142, q4: 205→163, q5: 245→163) fully resolved the failure. longest_correct_rate=0.00.

weakest_questions.distractor: The machine connects to the internet each time it answers a question and searches for matching examples to copy from web pages.
weakest_questions.reason: Not a common beginner misconception grounded in an introduced concept, but not score-1 absurd — a zero-background learner could plausibly imagine this. Score raised from 1 to 2 on re-assessment against the fresher-zero-background audience baseline.

weakest_questions.distractor: The mathematical concept of neural networks had not yet been discovered before 2010 — the key equations were only published in the early 2010s.
weakest_questions.reason: Contradicts corpus explicitly (neural network math existed since the 1940s–50s); weak for an attentive reader but not score-1 absurd for a zero-background learner who has not yet internalised that timeline.

coverage_map.lo_text: Describe in plain language what each of the three main phases of AI history tried to do and why each phase emerged.
Explain why symbolic AI succeeded in narrow tasks but struggled to scale to real-world complexity.
Explain what it means for a machine to 'learn from data' instead of following hand-written rules.
Identify at least two real-world milestones — such as Deep Blue or AlexNet — and place them in the correct phase of AI history.
Explain why deep learning became possible in the 2010s when it had not been possible earlier.

beginner_fairness_and_scope.notes: All stems are plain-language and readable without outside knowledge. Acronyms spelled out (AI, ANN, GPU). All questions test concepts explicitly covered in this topic's corpus (§5.1–5.4, §9–10). No 3.2+ content tested. The 'transformer' reference in q5 distractor d3 is a plausible wrong claim — the corpus mentions transformer as a 2017 milestone in a table, so the name is in-scope; answering correctly does not require knowing what a transformer is.

verdict: Pass — all five checks clean. Positional bias: max_share=0.40 (at boundary, non-strict threshold, pass). Length bias: longest_correct_rate=0.00, all targeted fixes verified effective. Distractor plausibility: absurd_rate=0.00. Objective coverage: all 5 LOs covered, no orphan questions. Beginner-fairness and scope: no out-of-scope questions, no unfair distractors.
